unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you will learn how to talk about emotions (how we feel), behaviour (how we act), and identity (who we are and where our family comes from). You will read about children who feel nervous, curious, proud, and shy — and who learn to be gentle, polite, and generous to others. You will practise saying how you feel, giving kind advice, and describing what makes your own family special. By the end, you will be ready to understand this unit's story, “The Day of the Play,” in which a girl called Nadia finds her courage on stage.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time — never all at once!
Read the Story file, “The Day of the Play,” and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned, and get ready for Unit 8.

visual.alt: Children expressing emotions, behaviour and identity respectfully

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling worried or a little afraid about something that might happen.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I felt nervous before my big test at school.
practiceSentences: I felt nervous before my big test at school.
Sami's hands shook because he was nervous about the recital.
Do new places make you feel nervous sometimes?
"Everyone gets nervous before a race," said the coach kindly.
I felt nervous when the dentist called my name.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - e - r - v - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'nervous', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling very worried, with lots of worried thoughts.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was anxious about being in the dark.
practiceSentences: She was anxious about being in the dark.
Samira felt anxious as the storm clouds gathered overhead.
Why were you anxious about the spelling test?
"When I feel anxious, I take deep breaths," said Maya.
I was anxious about the results until Father hugged me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - x - i - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'anxious', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very, very scared.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.
practiceSentences: I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.
The kitten was terrified of the roaring vacuum cleaner.
What made you so terrified during the film?
"I am terrified of heights," admitted Uncle Omar.
I felt terrified when the lights went out suddenly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - r - r - i - f - i - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'terrified', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not sure that something is true or will happen.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He felt doubtful about winning the game.
practiceSentences: He felt doubtful about winning the game.
Maya looked doubtful when Theo said he saw a falcon.
Why are you doubtful that the seeds will grow?
"The weather looks doubtful for our picnic," said Mother.
I was doubtful about the new recipe, but it tasted great.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - u - b - t - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'doubtful', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Really wanting to know or learn something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The curious cat looked inside the open box.
practiceSentences: The curious cat looked inside the open box.
The curious child asked questions about everything in the museum.
Are you curious about what lives inside the coral reef?
"Stay curious and keep asking questions," said Mrs Ali.
I was curious about the strange noise in the attic.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - u - r - i - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'curious', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling happy about something you did or who you are.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am proud of my drawing because I worked hard on it.
practiceSentences: I am proud of my drawing because I worked hard on it.
Father felt proud when Adam recited the whole surah.
Are you proud of the garden you planted?
"We are proud of your honesty," said the head teacher.
I felt proud when my little sister read her first book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - u - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'proud', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and soft in the way you act; not rough or loud.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She gave the puppy a gentle hug.
practiceSentences: She gave the puppy a gentle hug.
Theo was gentle when he held the baby chick.
Can you be gentle while brushing the cat?
"Use a gentle voice in the library," said the librarian.
My grandmother's gentle hands wrapped my scraped knee carefully.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - e - n - t - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'gentle', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Showing good manners and being kind to others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is polite to say 'please' and 'thank you.'
practiceSentences: It is polite to say 'please' and 'thank you.'
The polite boy held the door open for his teacher.
What is the polite way to ask for help?
"Being polite costs nothing at all," Grandfather always says.
I wrote a polite thank-you note to Aunt Maryam.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - l - i - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'polite', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Happy to share or give to others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The generous boy gave his snack to a friend.
practiceSentences: The generous boy gave his snack to a friend.
The generous baker gave extra bread to the poor family.
Who was generous enough to share their umbrella today?
"A generous heart is never empty," said the teacher.
My generous friend gave me half of her sandwich.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - e - n - e - r - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'generous', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Only caring about yourself and not about other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It was selfish to take all the cookies and not share.
practiceSentences: It was selfish to take all the cookies and not share.
The selfish king in the story kept all the water.
Is it selfish to want the swing all break time?
"Sharing stops us from being selfish," said Mother.
I felt selfish after hiding the coloured pencils, so I apologised.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - l - f - i - s - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'selfish', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not joking or silly; important and needing attention.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She had a serious look on her face.
practiceSentences: She had a serious look on her face.
The doctor wore a serious face while reading the chart.
Why did the meeting sound so serious today?
"This is a serious matter, children," said the head teacher.
I become serious when I recite in front of the class.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - r - i - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'serious', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Funny, or making people laugh.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher told a humorous story, and everyone laughed.
practiceSentences: The teacher told a humorous story, and everyone laughed.
Grandfather told a humorous tale about a runaway goat.
Which humorous book made you laugh the most?
"Your humorous poem won first prize," announced Mrs Ali.
I drew a humorous cartoon of a cat wearing boots.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - u - m - o - r - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'humorous', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very strong, powerful, or angry.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The fierce wind blew the leaves off the trees.
practiceSentences: The fierce wind blew the leaves off the trees.
A fierce storm rattled the windows all night long.
How does a lion's fierce roar warn other animals?
"That fierce wind nearly took my hat," laughed Grandfather.
Our team faced fierce competition in the final match.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - i - e - r - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fierce', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very bad or cruel.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The story had an evil villain who wanted to hurt people.
practiceSentences: The story had an evil villain who wanted to hurt people.
The evil wizard in the tale locked away the sunshine.
Why do stories often end with the evil character losing?
"Good deeds can defeat evil plans," said the storyteller.
I cheered when the evil pirate's ship sailed away defeated.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - v - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'evil', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Quiet and a little afraid to talk to other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The shy girl hid behind her mother.
practiceSentences: The shy girl hid behind her mother.
The shy new student ate lunch alone at first.
Were you shy on your first day of school?
"Do not be shy; come join our game," said Theo.
I was too shy to sing, so I hummed quietly instead.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'shy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Belonging to a group of people who share the same culture, language, or history.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In our class we celebrate many ethnic traditions.
practiceSentences: In our class we celebrate many ethnic traditions.
The festival displayed ethnic dishes from ten different countries.
Which ethnic dress will you wear for the celebration?
"Our market sells ethnic fabrics from many lands," said Aunty.
I love the ethnic patterns on my grandmother's shawl.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - t - h - n - i - c
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'ethnic', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone or something come closer.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bright flowers attract many bees.
practiceSentences: The bright flowers attract many bees.
Bright feeders attract many birds to the school garden.
Why do sweet smells attract so many insects?
"Magnets attract some metals but not others," explained Mrs Ali.
My shiny marbles attract attention at every break time.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - t - t - r - a - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'attract', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that might be dangerous or have a bad result.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Riding a bike without a helmet is risky.
practiceSentences: Riding a bike without a helmet is risky.
Crossing the stream on wet stones is very risky.
Why is it risky to swim right after eating?
"Climbing that old wall is too risky," warned Father.
I avoided the risky shortcut and took the safe path.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - i - s - k - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'risky', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Happening often, or many times.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We take frequent breaks during our lessons.
practiceSentences: We take frequent breaks during our lessons.
Buses to the city are frequent on market days.
How frequent are the trains on a Friday morning?
"Frequent practice makes your handwriting neat," said Mrs Ali.
My frequent visits to the library filled my reading card.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - e - q - u - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'frequent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A machine that washes dirty plates, cups, and spoons.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We put the dirty plates in the dishwasher after dinner.
practiceSentences: We put the dirty plates in the dishwasher after dinner.
The dishwasher hummed quietly while the family drank tea.
Who will load the dishwasher after dinner tonight?
"The dishwasher saves us time on busy nights," said Mother.
I unloaded the dishwasher as my morning job this week.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - h - w - a - s - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'dishwasher', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name many feelings and describe them, using words like nervous, anxious, proud, and curious.
Describe how people behave, using words like gentle, selfish, generous, and polite.
Talk about experiences in your life using the present perfect (“I have felt nervous before.”).
Talk about plans using the present continuous for the future (“We are visiting a fair tomorrow.”).
Tell about the past using the past simple (“She felt anxious last night.”).
Give advice and safety tips using imperatives and modal verbs (“Always be polite.” / “You should be gentle.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer questions about feelings (“Have you ever felt proud?”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about your family's ethnic background and a tradition you are proud of.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The Day Before the Test
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Samira sat quietly at the table. Tomorrow was her big spelling test, and she felt nervous. Her thoughts were anxious ones: “What if I forget the words?” Her older brother Idris saw her worried face. He was always gentle with her. “Don't be so hard on yourself,” he said kindly. “You have studied every day. I have felt nervous before a test too, and it was fine.” Samira was still doubtful, but she felt a little braver. After sunset, Mama brought her warm milk and sat with her. “Be polite to your worry,” Mama said with a smile, “then tell it to go to sleep.” Samira laughed. The next day she felt proud — she remembered every single word.

readings.title: Where My Family Comes From
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My name is Amal, and I am proud of who I am. My family is from a small town near the coast. We speak Somali at home and English at school. On special days, my grandmother tells stories about her childhood, and Mama cooks sweet xalwo for our guests. My friends come from many ethnic groups — some families are from the city, some from farming villages, and some from other countries. We are all different, and I think that is wonderful. Being kind and generous to each other is the most important thing. My grandmother always says, “A guest is a gift.”

readings.title: Getting Ready for the Play
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Good morning, class. Tomorrow is our school play, and I know some of you feel nervous. That is completely normal! Remember three things. First, you have practised hard, so you are ready. Second, be gentle and polite to each other backstage — don't be selfish with the costumes. Third, if you make a small mistake, just smile and keep going. I am so proud of every one of you.”

readings.title: A Chat About Feelings
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Selma: “How do you feel today, Hannah?”
Sophia: “I feel curious! We are visiting a cultural fair tomorrow. Have you ever been to one?”
Selma: “Yes, I have. I went last year, and I felt so proud to show my family's food. Why are you curious?”
Sophia: “Because I have never tried food from so many different places before!”

readings.title: The Day of the Play
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Nadia stood behind the school curtain, holding her costume. She looked out at the rows of chairs, all filled with parents. Her hands shook a little.
“I'm so nervous,” she whispered.
“You'll be great,” said her friend Sami. “You've practised every single line.”
Amal tried to smile. This was the first time she had ever been in a school play. She had the main part — the kind queen who helps people in need. Her teacher had told her, “Nadia, you should be proud. You've worked so hard.”
Still, Nadia felt anxious. What if she forgot her words? What if the lights were too bright?
“You mustn't be so hard on yourself,” said Sami. “Just be gentle and speak politely, like the queen in the story.”
Amal nodded. “Thanks. I'll try.”
Earlier that week, the whole class had worked hard on the costumes. Sami wore a lion mask. Nadia's little brother, Isaac, came to watch with their parents.
At home the night before, Isaac had told her, “Don't be shy, Nadia. You always help me with my homework. You can talk in front of people.”
Amal had laughed. “Talking to you is much easier!”
“But you've done harder things,” Isaac said. “Remember the fierce rainstorm? You helped Mama carry the water buckets in the wind. That was risky!”
She smiled now as she remembered that day.
The lights in the hall came on. The show began.
First, the lion walked onto the stage. Sami frequently made the crowd laugh with his funny, humorous roars. The queen had to stop him.
Amal stepped out. Her voice was quiet at first, but it grew stronger and stronger.
“You must not act wild,” she told the lion, in a serious voice. “We must be polite and kind.”
The audience clapped.
As the play went on, Nadia forgot to feel anxious. She looked at the people in front of her — mothers in colourful ethnic dresses, children from many different backgrounds, and fathers with proud smiles. She felt calm.
At the end, the queen gives her crown to a poor child. Nadia bent down and said softly, “This crown is not just for me. A good leader must be generous.”
The whole crowd stood up and cheered.
Backstage, the teacher clapped. “Nadia, you were amazing!”
Amal grinned. “I don't feel shy anymore.”
After the play, the students helped tidy up. Nadia and Sami went to wash the paint brushes in the art-room sink. “Let's put these in the dishwasher,” said Sami, pointing at the sink.
“You know that's not a real dishwasher!” Nadia said, laughing.
Outside, a new student stood alone near the wall. She looked terrified. Nadia walked over and said, “Hi, I'm Nadia. Did you like the play?”
The girl nodded but did not speak.
“She seems doubtful,” whispered Sami.
“She's probably just curious, or maybe nervous,” Nadia said. “Let's be kind. It would be selfish to leave her on her own.”
They brought her a chair and a cool drink.
“You were very gentle on the stage,” the girl said at last.
“Thank you!” Nadia smiled. “You're welcome to play with us next time.”
That night, Nadia wrote in her notebook:
“Today I was scared, but I found my courage. I used a gentle voice and I spoke politely. I have felt anxious before, but now I know it's okay. I have also been shy, but helping someone made me feel better. I have learned that being kind is much better than being selfish. I'm not just proud of the play — I'm proud of how I behaved.”
Her mother walked in. “You're still awake?”
“I'm writing about my day,” Nadia said.
“You should be proud,” her mother said gently. “You've grown in so many ways.”
Amal closed her notebook and turned off the light. She smiled and whispered, “I'm ready for tomorrow.”

grammar.title: Present Perfect (for life experiences)
grammar.explanation: We use the present perfect to talk about things we have done or felt at some time in our life, when the exact time does not matter. It connects the past to now. Saying “I have felt nervous before” shows the feeling happened, and it still helps us understand ourselves today.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this pattern: subject + have / has + past participle.
Use have with: I, you, we, they.  →  “I have travelled to many places.”
Use has with: he, she, it.  →  “She has never been shy.”
The past participle is the third form of a verb. Regular verbs add -ed (play → played). Irregular verbs change (go → gone, eat → eaten, be → been).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I have eat lunch.”  ✗
Fix: Use the past participle, not the base verb: “I have eaten lunch.”  ✓
Mistake: “She have seen that film.”  ✗   Fix: “She has seen that film.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of have / has as a bridge between your past and now. He, she, and it always take has.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence using the present perfect. | I __________ (never / try) sushi. | He __________ (see) that film three times. | They __________ (travel) to Kenya. | She __________ (not / be) to a concert.

grammar.title: Present Continuous for Future Plans
grammar.explanation: The present continuous is not only for things happening right now. We also use it to talk about future plans that we have already arranged. It tells people what we have decided to do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this pattern: subject + am / is / are + verb-ing + a future time word.
Use am with I, is with he / she / it, and are with you / we / they.
Add a time word for the future, like tomorrow, next week, or on Friday.
Example: “We are visiting a cultural fair tomorrow.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I am go to the fair tomorrow.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the verb: “I am going to the fair tomorrow.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A present-continuous future is like a promise you have already made. Always add a future time word so people know you mean later, not now.
grammar.practice: Put the verb in the present continuous for a future plan. | I __________ (visit) my grandparents this weekend. | They __________ (have) a picnic next Sunday. | She __________ (join) the football team next month. | We __________ (watch) a film tonight.

grammar.title: Past Simple (for finished actions)
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about actions that started and finished in the past. It helps us tell stories and share what happened — like “She felt anxious last night.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs add -ed:  play → played,  walk → walked.
Irregular verbs change in their own way:  go → went,  see → saw,  eat → ate,  feel → felt.
Add a past time word: yesterday, last night, last week.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She feels anxious last night.”  ✗
Fix: Change the verb to the past: “She felt anxious last night.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Yesterday's actions use -ed for regular verbs. For irregular verbs, learn a few each day.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb. | I __________ (play) football after school yesterday. | They __________ (go) to the zoo last weekend. | She __________ (feel) tired after the party. | He __________ (eat) rice for dinner yesterday.

grammar.title: Imperatives for Advice and Safety
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are sentences that give advice, instructions, or safety tips. They help us tell people clearly what to do, or what not to do, to stay safe and be kind.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base form of the verb: “Be polite.” “Listen to your teacher.”
For negative advice, start with Don't: “Don't be selfish.”
You do not need to say “you” — it is already understood.
Add Always or Please to sound friendly: “Always say thank you.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You be polite.”  ✗   Fix: Just say “Be polite.”  ✓
Mistake: “Don't be selfishly.”  ✗   Fix: Keep the base word: “Don't be selfish.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of imperatives as friendly commands — a helpful nudge in the right direction. No “you” needed!
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with the correct imperative. | __________ (be) careful when you cross the road. | __________ (not / touch) the hot stove! | __________ (always / say) thank you. | __________ (not / interrupt) when someone is speaking.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs for Advice (must, mustn't, should)
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs like must, mustn't, and should are helping verbs that give advice or strong suggestions. They let us tell people what is necessary or what is a good idea.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this pattern: subject + modal verb + base form of the verb.
must = strong advice, very important:  “You must wash your hands before eating.”
mustn't = do not do this:  “You mustn't run in the hallway.”
should = a good idea, softer advice:  “You should be gentle with others.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You must goes outside.”  ✗   Fix: Use the base verb: “You must go outside.”  ✓
Mistake: Using must for gentle advice. Use should instead: “You should relax.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: must is a strong “you really need to”, mustn't is a warning “stop!”, and should is a friendly “it's a good idea”.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with must, mustn't, or should. | You __________ eat your vegetables to stay healthy. | You __________ leave your toys on the floor; pick them up! | You __________ say kind things to your friends. | You __________ talk to strangers you do not know.

grammar.title: Question Forms + Descriptive Collocations
grammar.explanation: Questions help us learn about each other and the world. Good learners ask good questions! In this unit we ask people how they feel and what they have done.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Yes / No questions start with a verb:  “Do you like football?” “Are you happy?”
Wh- questions start with a question word (who, what, where, when, why, how):  “How do you feel today?”
For experiences, use the present perfect:  “Have you ever felt proud?”
Always end a question with a question mark (?).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You are happy?”  ✗   Fix: Swap the order: “Are you happy?”  ✓
Mistake: “How do you feel today” (no mark)  ✗   Fix: “How do you feel today?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Each question word is like a key that unlocks new information. Start with a question word or a verb, and never forget the question mark.
grammar.practice: Change each statement into a question. | You like football.  →  __________________________? | She is happy.  →  __________________________? | They went to the park.  →  __________________________? | Lesson 7:  Descriptive Collocations | What it means and why it matters | A collocation is a pair of words that often go together and sound natural. For feelings and behaviour, some adjectives are “best friends” with certain nouns, like anxious thoughts or a proud smile. Using them makes your writing richer. | How to form it | Choose a noun: thoughts. | Choose the adjective that fits it best: anxious. | Put them together: anxious thoughts. | Other good pairs: fierce debate, proud smile, gentle voice. | A common mistake | Mistake: “fierce thoughts” — this sounds strange.  ✗ | Fix: Choose an adjective that fits: “anxious thoughts.”  ✓  If a pair sounds odd, try another word. | Memory tip | When you find a pair that sounds natural, remember it as a special team of words. | Practice 7 | Choose the word that makes the natural collocation. | I had __________ (anxious / happy) thoughts before the exam. | The class had a __________ (fierce / soft) debate about the best game. | She gave me a __________ (sad / proud) smile after my presentation.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: How Do You Feel?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise talking about your feelings. Say a sentence for each one, and add a reason with because:
“Today I feel ______ because ______.”
“Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
“I have felt ______ before, when ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use a feelings word? Did you give a reason? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise how do you feel? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Give Kind Advice
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine a friend feels nervous or shy. Give them three pieces of kind advice using should, must, and an imperative:
“You should ______.”
“You must ______.”
“Always ______.”  /  “Don't ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise give kind advice and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Feelings Charades
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Choose a feelings word (like terrified, curious, or proud) but keep it secret.
Show the feeling with your face and body — no words!
The other person guesses: “Are you feeling ______?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise feelings charades and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: A Feelings Blog Post
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short blog post (four or five sentences) called “A Day I Felt ______.” Choose a feeling such as anxious, curious, or proud. Tell what happened, how you felt, and what you did. Try to use the past simple and one present perfect sentence.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a feelings blog post.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: A Mini-Guide: How to Be Kind at School
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a little guide titled “How to Be Polite and Kind at School.” Write five pieces of advice using imperatives and modal verbs. For example:
“Always say please and thank you.”
“You should share, and never be selfish.”
“Don't be rough — be gentle with younger children.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a mini-guide: how to be kind at school.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: Proud of My Identity
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three or four sentences about your family's background or a tradition you are proud of. You could write about a special food, a language you speak, or a celebration like new year.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about proud of my identity.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: Match the Feeling
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each feeling word to what a person might say. Write the pairs.
terrified  —  a) “I really want to know what is inside that box!”
curious  —  b) “There is a huge spider on the wall!”
proud  —  c) “I worked so hard, and I finished it all by myself.”
shy  —  d) “I don't want to speak in front of everyone.”
Answers: 1-b   2-a   3-c   4-d
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Behaviour: Kind or Unkind?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write kind or unkind for each action.
Sharing your snack with a friend who has none.
Taking all the toys and not letting others play.
Speaking gently to a new student.
Laughing at someone who made a mistake.
Answers: 1. kind (generous)   2. unkind (selfish)   3. kind (gentle/polite)   4. unkind
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Fill in the Feeling
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with a feelings word from this unit.
Before the big test, I felt ______.
When I saw the huge dog, I was ______.
I finished my project all by myself, so I felt ______.
The new girl was too ______ to talk to anyone.
Sample answers: 1. nervous / anxious   2. terrified   3. proud   4. shy
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Word Building: Adjectives to Adverbs
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Many feelings and behaviour words can become adverbs. Add -ly (and watch the spelling!).
nervous  →  ______
polite  →  ______
gentle  →  ______
frequent  →  ______
Answers: 1. nervously   2. politely   3. gently (drop the e, add -ly)   4. frequently
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: A Feelings Blog Post and a recording of Speaking 1: How Do You Feel?. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'nervous' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling worried or a little afraid about something that might happen. | Not sure that something is true or will happen. | Really wanting to know or learn something. | Very, very scared.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Feeling worried or a little afraid about something that might happen.
quizzes.explanation: I felt nervous before my big test at school.

quizzes.question: What does 'anxious' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling happy about something you did or who you are. | Feeling very worried, with lots of worried thoughts. | Not sure that something is true or will happen. | Really wanting to know or learn something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Feeling very worried, with lots of worried thoughts.
quizzes.explanation: She was anxious about being in the dark.

quizzes.question: What does 'terrified' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling happy about something you did or who you are. | Kind and soft in the way you act; not rough or loud. | Really wanting to know or learn something. | Very, very scared.
quizzes.explanation: I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.

quizzes.question: What does 'doubtful' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling happy about something you did or who you are. | Kind and soft in the way you act; not rough or loud. | Not sure that something is true or will happen. | Showing good manners and being kind to others.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not sure that something is true or will happen.
quizzes.explanation: He felt doubtful about winning the game.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Day Before the Test': Tomorrow was her big spelling test, and she felt _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Tomorrow was her big spelling test, and she felt nervous.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Day Before the Test': Her thoughts were _____ ones: “What if I forget the words?” Her older brother Adam saw her worried face.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Her thoughts were anxious ones: “What if I forget the words?” Her older brother Adam saw her worried face.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Day Before the Test': I have felt nervous before a test too, and it was fine.” Samira was still _____, but she felt a little braver.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: I have felt nervous before a test too, and it was fine.” Samira was still doubtful, but she felt a little braver.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Chat About Feelings': Selma: “How do you feel today, Hannah?” Sophia: “I feel _____!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Selma: “How do you feel today, Hannah?” Sophia: “I feel curious!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Day Before the Test': The next day she felt _____ — she remembered every single word.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The next day she felt proud — she remembered every single word.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Getting Ready for the Play': Second, be _____ and polite to each other backstage — don't be selfish with the costumes.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Second, be gentle and polite to each other backstage — don't be selfish with the costumes.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Feeling worried or a little afraid about something that might happen. I felt nervous before my big test at school.
Feeling very worried, with lots of worried thoughts. She was anxious about being in the dark.
Very, very scared. I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.
Not sure that something is true or will happen. He felt doubtful about winning the game.
Really wanting to know or learn something. The curious cat looked inside the open box.
Feeling happy about something you did or who you are. I am proud of my drawing because I worked hard on it.
Kind and soft in the way you act; not rough or loud. She gave the puppy a gentle hug.
Showing good manners and being kind to others. It is polite to say 'please' and 'thank you.'
Happy to share or give to others. The generous boy gave his snack to a friend.
Only caring about yourself and not about other people. It was selfish to take all the cookies and not share.
